data.title: CenterPoint Energy Earnings Call Highlights Growth Path
data.description:
Centerpoint Energy Inc ((CNP)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

CenterPoint Energy’s latest earnings call struck a distinctly upbeat tone, with management emphasizing strong 2025 execution, accelerating Houston load growth and a larger, well-funded capital plan. While higher O&M, interest expense and slightly pressured credit metrics created some near-term drag, executives stressed that tax relief, securitizations and asset sales should more than offset these headwinds over time.

Robust Earnings, Dividend Growth Underpin Investor Confidence

CenterPoint reported 2025 non‑GAAP EPS of $1.76, up 9% versus 2024, alongside Q4 non‑GAAP EPS of $0.45 and GAAP EPS of $1.60 for the full year. Dividend per share also rose 9%, signaling confidence in cash flow durability and reinforcing the company’s positioning as a growth‑oriented regulated utility.

Reaffirmed 2026 Outlook and Ambitious Long‑Term EPS Targets

Management reaffirmed 2026 non‑GAAP EPS guidance of $1.89–$1.91, with the midpoint implying roughly 8% growth over 2025 results. Looking beyond 2026, CenterPoint is targeting 7%–9% annual non‑GAAP EPS growth through 2028 and expects to sustain that 7%–9% trajectory each year through 2035.

Houston Load Boom Pulls Demand Forecasts Forward

The company now projects a 50% increase in Houston peak load by 2029, equivalent to about 10 GW of additional demand and two years earlier than prior forecasts. The pipeline includes 2.5 GW already under construction, 5 GW firmly committed for energization by 2028 and roughly 3 GW of ordinary course growth.

Capital Plan Expanded Beyond $65 Billion With New 765 kV Line

CenterPoint raised its ten‑year capital investment plan to more than $65 billion by adding $500 million for a third 765 kV import line to support Houston’s growth and reliability. Management also identified over $10 billion of additional upside opportunities, underscoring a deep backlog of potential regulated infrastructure projects.

Outperformance on 2025 CapEx and Confirmation of 2026 Spend

The company invested $5.4 billion in 2025, exceeding its revised $5.3 billion plan and demonstrating strong execution on grid and system upgrades. For 2026, CenterPoint reaffirmed capital expenditures of $6.8 billion, which feed into an expected rate‑base growth rate above 11% through 2030.

Regulatory Progress and Ohio Exit Support Strategic Focus

CenterPoint secured a final order in its Ohio gas rate case, with an approved revenue requirement of about $53.1 million and an allowed ROE of 9.79%. The company expects to close the sale of its Ohio business in the fourth quarter, generating roughly $800 million of net cash proceeds and simplifying its regulatory footprint.

Financing Moves Bolster Liquidity and De‑Risk Recovery

The utility priced around $1.2 billion in securitization bonds tied to hurricane recovery, with plans to use proceeds to repay a $500 million term loan and reduce commercial paper. Combined with expected cash from the Ohio sale, these actions should materially enhance liquidity and support funding of the expanded capital program.

Tax Ruling Transforms Cash Tax and Credit Profile

New U.S. Treasury guidance on corporate AMT repairs deductions cut CenterPoint’s projected annual cash tax burden from about $150 million to near zero through 2035. Management believes this improves credit metrics by 60–70 basis points and could support roughly $1 billion of incremental customer‑driven CapEx without issuing new equity.

Reliability Gains and Hosting Capacity Benefit Houston Customers

CenterPoint highlighted a roughly 100 million‑minute reduction in outage time in Greater Houston as grid investments begin to pay off. Management also noted that using 5 GW of existing hosting capacity could lower average residential delivery charges by more than 2% versus the 2025 bill and help keep rates essentially flat through 2028.

Higher O&M From Accelerated Resiliency Work

Operating and maintenance costs were a $0.02 headwind to Q4 results as the company pulled some reliability and resiliency projects originally slated for 2026 into 2025. While this raises near‑term expense, management framed it as front‑loaded investment aimed at hardening the system and meeting the region’s faster‑than‑expected load growth.

Interest Expense and Credit Metrics Face Near‑Term Pressure

Higher interest expense reduced Q4 earnings by $0.05, driven by about $3.3 billion in incremental debt required to fund the capital program and hurricane recovery. Adjusted FFO‑to‑debt stood at 13.8% at year‑end, slightly below the desired cushion, though management expects improvement toward roughly 15% as tax savings and securitizations flow through.

Rate Recovery Timing Creates 2025 Earnings Cadence Hurdles

The company noted that 2025 year‑over‑year rate recovery is affected by delays in several interim recovery mechanisms, many of which kick in during the second half. This creates an uneven earnings cadence versus prior expectations, but does not change the underlying economics of the investments once the mechanisms are fully in place.

Non‑Recurring Charges From Asset Dispositions and Temporary Generation

GAAP EPS included an $0.11 charge tied to the disposition of goodwill associated with Louisiana and Mississippi gas businesses. In addition, $0.07 of depreciation related to large temporary generation units weighed on results, and management plans to market those units for sublease or sale, adding some execution and timing uncertainty.

Forward‑Looking Guidance Anchored by Growth, CapEx and Tax Tailwinds

CenterPoint’s outlook remains firmly growth‑oriented, with 2026 non‑GAAP EPS guided to $1.89–$1.91 and long‑term EPS growth targeted at 7%–9% annually through 2035. Management expects more than $65 billion in capital spending, over 11% rate‑base growth, accelerated Houston load, favorable AMT tax treatment and securitization‑backed financing to support earnings, dividend expansion and gradually improving credit metrics.

The call painted a picture of a utility leaning into a rare combination of robust demand growth, regulatory visibility and improved tax economics. While investors must weigh the impact of higher debt, near‑term O&M, and timing of recoveries, management’s detailed plan suggests that CenterPoint is well positioned to convert its Houston growth opportunity and outsized capital plan into sustained EPS and dividend growth over the next decade.

data.title: Safe Bulkers Inc. Balances Resilience With Profit Pressure
data.description:
Safe Bulkers Inc ((SB)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Safe Bulkers Inc.’s latest earnings call painted a cautiously optimistic picture, blending solid financial resilience with emerging pressure on profitability. Management highlighted strong liquidity, steady free cash flow and a high‑quality fleet, yet acknowledged softer earnings, rising costs and macro headwinds that could weigh on near‑term performance.

Dividend Declaration and Capital Returns

The board declared a $0.05 per share dividend for the fourth quarter of 2025, extending a pattern of shareholder distributions. Management framed the payout as a signal of confidence in ongoing cash generation and disciplined capital allocation despite a slightly weaker earnings quarter.

Robust Liquidity and Financial Flexibility

Safe Bulkers reported about $167 million in cash as of mid‑February 2026 and roughly $218 million of undrawn revolving credit. This combined liquidity of around $385 million gives the company ample room to weather volatility, pursue fleet investments and sustain shareholder returns.

Revenue Backlog and Charter Coverage

The company emphasized strong earnings visibility through its contracted book, particularly in larger vessels. Its Capesize ships earn roughly $24,000 per day on average, driving a backlog above $130 million within more than $178 million of total contracted revenue.

Seventeenth Straight Quarter of Free Cash Flow

Management underscored that this was the 17th consecutive quarter of free cash flow generation, a notable achievement in a cyclical industry. The streak reflects a focus on cost discipline, prudent leverage and a balanced chartering strategy across market conditions.

Fleet Quality and Renewal Strategy

Safe Bulkers continues to lean on fleet quality as a competitive edge, with about 80% of its vessels built in Japan versus roughly 40% for the global fleet. An eight‑ship newbuilding program, mostly Japanese, aims to enhance fuel efficiency and CII performance while supporting long‑term competitiveness.

Conservative Balance Sheet and Low Leverage

The company stressed its conservative balance sheet, highlighting low net debt intensity at about €8.4 million per vessel. This modest leverage profile offers flexibility to navigate downturns, fund renewal and potentially capitalize on opportunities when asset prices are attractive.

Market Demand Pockets and Growth Drivers

Management cited external forecasts pointing to modest but positive dry‑bulk demand growth of roughly 2%–3% in 2026. Grains are expected to expand 5%–6%, while India’s projected GDP growth near 6.4% could bolster ton‑mile demand and partly offset softness in traditional China‑linked trades.

EBITDA Softness Signals Lower Profitability

Adjusted EBITDA slipped to $37.4 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 from $40.7 million a year earlier, an 8.1% decline. The drop underscores a tougher operating environment as weaker freight dynamics and higher expenses erode some of the benefits of the company’s strong positioning.

Earnings Per Share Slightly Weaker

Adjusted EPS for the quarter came in at $0.14 versus $0.15 in the prior‑year period, a 6.7% decrease. While the decline is modest, it highlights that earnings momentum has cooled even as the company continues to manage its capital structure and share count efficiently.

Rising Operating Costs Pressure Margins

Daily vessel operating expenses climbed about 13% year‑on‑year to $5,686 in the fourth quarter, with core OpEx excluding certain delivery charges up around 6%. If this trend persists, higher running costs could compress margins and dilute the impact of healthy charter coverage and backlog.

Macro Volatility and China‑Linked Risks

Management flagged heightened volatility stemming from geopolitical tensions and a mixed macro backdrop, especially in China. Elevated Chinese port inventories, a weak property sector and policy shifts were all cited as factors that could dampen seaborne demand and weigh on freight rates.

Soft Patches in Coal and Iron Ore Flows

Key commodity flows showed signs of strain, with coal shipments projected to shrink 1%–2% in 2026 and global coal demand seen declining further over 2025–2027. Iron ore seaborne growth is expected to be modest at about 1%, challenged by already high Chinese stockpiles that limit upside for bulk carriers.

Fleet Renewal Timing and Shipyard Constraints

The company pointed to constraints in executing fleet renewal, as high‑quality Japanese secondhand tonnage remains scarce and major shipyards are effectively booked through 2028. That pushes newbuilding delivery slots into 2029, complicating near‑term growth and modernization plans.

Regulation and Fuel Transition Uncertainty

Evolving environmental rules and uncertainty around future fuel standards add another layer of risk for dry‑bulk owners. With only a small portion of the order book designed for alternative fuels, Safe Bulkers and peers must navigate possible compliance costs and operational changes as regulations tighten into the next decade.

Forward Guidance and Market Outlook

Management expects dry‑bulk supply and demand to remain broadly balanced in 2026, with fleet growth around 2.5% and deliveries near 3% against demand of 2%–3%. Within this framework, grains and minor bulks should support ton‑miles, while coal drags, and the company will lean on its $178 million revenue backlog, low net debt and high‑quality, 10.4‑year‑old fleet to manage through.

Safe Bulkers’ call ultimately blended resilience with realism, as strong liquidity, charter coverage and fleet quality offset weaker earnings and rising costs. Investors are left with a story of a well‑positioned owner in a balanced but uncertain market, where disciplined capital management and careful fleet renewal will be key to protecting returns.

data.title: Yeti Holdings Balances Growth Ambitions With Margin Strain
data.description:
Yeti Holdings ((YETI)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Yeti Holdings’ latest earnings call painted a mixed but generally constructive picture for investors. Management highlighted solid sales growth, especially overseas, strong free cash flow, and aggressive share repurchases, underscoring confidence in the brand. However, tariffs, higher spending, and pressured margins weighed on profits, setting up a near-term trade‑off between growth and earnings.

Q4 Revenue Delivers Solid Growth

Yeti posted adjusted Q4 net sales of $583.7 million, up 5% year over year and the strongest quarter of 2025. The performance demonstrated resilient demand across the portfolio despite a tougher consumer backdrop and ongoing macro uncertainty.

Drinkware Rebound Led by Innovation

Drinkware revenue rose 6% in Q4 to $380 million, fueled by new products and strong international traction. While U.S. Drinkware sales were flat, the global portfolio showed clear improvement, suggesting that innovation and geographic diversification are offsetting domestic softness.

Coolers & Equipment Show Steady Momentum

Coolers & Equipment sales increased 2% to $192 million, supported by robust sell‑through in soft coolers and bags such as DayTrip and Camino. Management emphasized that demand outpaced available supply in some of these items, signaling underlying category health even as top‑line growth remained modest.

International Expansion Outperforms

International revenue surged 25% to $136 million, lifting its mix to 23% of Q4 sales from 20% a year ago. Growth was broad‑based across Europe, Japan, Australia, Canada, and wider Asia, reinforcing Yeti’s progress in building a global lifestyle brand beyond its U.S. core.

Balanced Growth Across DTC and Wholesale

Direct‑to‑consumer sales climbed 5% to $394 million, while wholesale revenue gained 6% to $189 million. The company pointed to strength across Amazon, owned e‑commerce, corporate orders, and its retail stores, underscoring diversified channel growth and a healthier mix than in prior years.

Cash Generation Fuels Share Buybacks

For 2025, Yeti generated $212 million in free cash flow, providing ample room for capital returns. The company repurchased $125 million of stock in Q4 and about $298 million over the year, shrinking the share count by roughly 14% over two years and signaling confidence in long‑term value.

Brand and Product Engine Accelerates

Management spotlighted an expanding innovation pipeline, including Silo jugs, the Yonder shaker, DayTrip snack boxes, Scala packs, and the GoBox One, alongside broader Drinkware platforms. Marketing punch is also increasing, with roughly 240 million impressions from the holiday campaign and a goal of around 400 million for spring 2026.

Tariffs Hit Gross Margins Hard

Adjusted gross margin fell to 58.4% in Q4, down 180 basis points, driven by about 310 basis points of gross pressure from higher tariffs. Looking ahead, 2026 guidance bakes in roughly 200 basis points of additional tariff impact and an estimated $0.35 unfavorable EPS drag versus 2025.

Profitability Slips Despite Revenue Growth

Adjusted operating income dropped 14% to $94.7 million, or 16.2% of sales, while adjusted net income slid 15% to $71.8 million. Adjusted EPS declined to $0.92 from $1.00, highlighting that top‑line gains were not enough to offset tariff costs and higher operating expenses.

Higher SG&A Reflects Investment Phase

Adjusted SG&A expanded 10% to $246 million and rose 190 basis points as a share of sales to 42.2%. Management linked the increase to heavier spending on marketing, technology, facilities, and global teams, framing it as necessary fuel for future growth rather than structural inefficiency.

Balance Sheet Impact from Buybacks

Yeti ended the year with $188 million in cash, down from $359 million, largely due to the nearly $298 million deployed on repurchases. Debt remained low at $74 million, leaving the balance sheet relatively conservative even after substantial cash returns to shareholders.

Supply Constraints Cap Near‑Term Upside

Sell‑in was held back by supply limitations in high‑demand products, especially DayTrip soft coolers and Camino bags, limiting wholesale replenishment. Management expects these constraints to ease as new production capacity comes online in the first half of 2026, which should better align inventory with demand.

Promotions and Cautious Wholesale Temper U.S. Growth

An elevated promotional environment in parts of Drinkware, combined with prudent ordering from U.S. wholesale partners, pressured domestic results. Inventories fell year over year, and U.S. Drinkware was flat in Q4, reflecting a more disciplined channel that may benefit margins once tariffs stabilize.

Margin Phasing to Be H1‑Weighted Downside

The company warned that 2026 margin pressure will be front‑loaded, with gross margin expected to be down roughly 300 basis points year over year in the first half, particularly in Q1. Operating margins are projected to fall about 500 basis points in H1 before recovering in the back half as price, mix, and cost actions take hold.

Tariff Relief Could Offer Upside Optionality

Management noted that current guidance assumes no tariff relief, despite ongoing legal and policy uncertainty that could change outcomes. This conservative stance leaves potential upside to earnings and margins if tariffs are reduced or refunded, but investors are being asked to underwrite the business without that benefit for now.

Guidance Signals Growth with Near‑Term Margin Strain

For 2026, Yeti forecasts net sales growth of 6%–8% with even quarterly pacing, led by high‑teens to 20% international growth and modest low‑ to mid‑single‑digit gains in the U.S. Coolers & Equipment should grow high single‑digit to low double‑digit, Drinkware mid single‑digit, wholesale slightly faster than DTC, and gross margin of 56%–57% implies about 90 basis points of decline at the midpoint.

Guidance Signals Growth with Near‑Term Margin Strain (cont.)

Adjusted SG&A is seen rising 3%–7%, resulting in an operating margin around 14.4%, roughly flat versus 2025 despite heavy H1 pressure. Adjusted EPS is projected at $2.77–$2.83, up 12%–14% even after a $0.35 net tariff headwind, supported by $60–$70 million of capex, $200–$225 million of free cash flow, and a further $100 million in planned buybacks.

Yeti’s earnings call underscored a business still delivering healthy revenue growth, powerful international expansion, and strong cash returns, even as tariffs and stepped‑up investments compress margins. For investors, the story is a classic trade‑off: accept near‑term earnings pressure in exchange for brand building, innovation, and global scale that management believes will drive stronger, more durable growth beyond 2026.

data.title: Radian Group Earnings Call: Core Strength, Bold Deal
data.description:
Radian Group Inc ((RDN)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Radian Group’s latest earnings call struck a notably upbeat tone, underscoring strong core performance in mortgage insurance, robust returns on equity and healthy book value growth. Management balanced this optimism with candid discussion of rising defaults and a sharp, but manageable, drop in holding company liquidity following its transformative Inigo acquisition.

Mortgage Insurance In‑Force Hits Record Levels

Radian’s mortgage insurance in‑force climbed to an all‑time high of $283 billion, up 3% from a year ago, as the company benefited from steady demand. New insurance written reached $55 billion in 2025, a 6% increase versus 2024, with Q4 NIW of $15.9 billion outpacing the prior‑year quarter’s $13.2 billion.

Strong Earnings and EPS Boosted by Buybacks

Net income from continuing operations reached $159 million in Q4, or $1.15 per share, and $618 million, or $4.39 per share, for the full year, reflecting resilient profitability. Earnings per share were further supported by an 8% year‑over‑year decline in share count, highlighting the accretive impact of active repurchases.

Solid Returns on Equity and Book Value Expansion

Return on equity remained attractive at 13.5% in Q4 and 13.1% for the full year, underscoring efficient capital deployment. Book value per share rose 13% year‑over‑year to $35.29, and management noted that dividends added roughly another 3% of value, reinforcing total shareholder return.

Revenue Growth, Premium Strength and Investment Income

Total revenues reached $301 million in Q4 and $1.2 billion for the year, supported by the highest level of net premiums earned in more than three years at $237 million in the quarter. The company’s $6.1 billion investment portfolio generated $249 million of net investment income in 2025, providing a meaningful earnings tailwind in a higher‑rate environment.

Credit Trends and Favorable Reserve Development

Credit performance stayed constructive, with Radian maintaining a 7.5% default‑to‑claim assumption on new defaults and recording a $57 million provision for Q4 new defaults. This was partially offset by $35 million of favorable reserve development, resulting in a net provision expense of $22 million as strong cure trends supported a 92.5% cumulative cure assumption.

Capital Returns and Share Repurchase Activity

Radian Guaranty returned $795 million to the holding company in 2025, enabling significant capital deployment to shareholders. Radian Group distributed about $576 million via dividends and buybacks, including the repurchase of roughly 13.5 million shares for $430 million, signaling confidence in intrinsic value.

Inigo Acquisition Positions Radian for Transformative Growth

The company closed its acquisition of specialty insurer Inigo for about $1.67 billion, funded entirely with existing liquidity rather than new equity. With Inigo’s estimated tangible equity at $1.16 billion, management paid roughly 1.4 times tangible book and expects the deal to double annual revenue, be accretive to EPS and deliver mid‑teens returns through the cycle.

Robust Capital Buffer and Conservative Leverage

Radian ended the year with a $1.6 billion PMIERs capital cushion, comfortably above regulatory requirements and reinforced by an excess‑of‑loss reinsurance deal on about $373 million of older policies. The leverage ratio declined to 18.3%, and management plans to keep it below 20% by year‑end 2026, preserving balance sheet flexibility.

Portfolio Simplification and Liquidity Rebuild Plan

The company is progressing with plans to divest its Mortgage Conduit, Title and Real Estate Services businesses by Q3 2026, streamlining its focus. Before closing Inigo, holding company liquidity stood at $1.8 billion, and management expects at least $600 million of dividends from Radian Guaranty in 2026 to restore liquidity and repay a $200 million credit facility draw.

Rising Defaults and Higher Default Inventory

Radian reported about 14,200 new defaults in Q4, pushing total defaults to roughly 25,000 loans and raising the portfolio default rate to 2.56%. Management framed the increase as largely driven by normal seasonality and seasoning of the larger in‑force book, but investors will watch closely to see whether this trend moderates.

Provision Expense Persists Despite Positive Reserve Releases

Even with $35 million of favorable reserve development in Q4, the company posted a net provision expense of $22 million due to higher provisioning on new defaults. This dynamic underscores how an expanding default inventory can still pressure earnings, even when prior‑period books exhibit strong cures and reserve releases.

Liquidity Tightness After Inigo Close

Following the Inigo purchase, holding company liquidity dropped sharply from $1.8 billion at year‑end to about $350 million, increasing near‑term dependence on subsidiary dividends. Management emphasized that expected cash flows from Radian Guaranty should comfortably rebuild liquidity, but the interim squeeze is a key risk factor for investors to monitor.

Inigo Integration and Accounting Uncertainties

Management acknowledged that purchase accounting for Inigo is not fully finalized, with the split between goodwill and intangible assets still being worked through. This means amortization expenses and some reported results will remain somewhat uncertain in the near term, though the integration itself is expected to be relatively light.

Limited Near‑Term Visibility Into Inigo Metrics

The company refrained from offering detailed forward guidance for Inigo, pointing only to its historical mid‑to‑high‑80s combined ratios as a directional benchmark. Investors will need to wait until the first combined reporting period for clearer insight into segment performance, earnings volatility and capital needs.

Persistency and Seasonality in Portfolio Performance

Quarterly persistency eased slightly to 82% amid higher refinance activity, but management still expects overall persistency to remain strong given current rate levels. They cautioned that both persistency and cure patterns could shift as newer vintages season, suggesting that credit and revenue trends may become more sensitive to macro conditions.

Guidance and Outlook Emphasize Accretion and Discipline

Looking ahead, Radian expects Inigo to roughly double group revenues and drive mid‑teens returns, with management estimating about $170 million of pretax incremental income and meaningful ROE accretion. The company aims to maintain a $1.6 billion PMIERs cushion, keep premium yields around 38 basis points, preserve high persistency, hold leverage below 20% and potentially resume opportunistic buybacks as liquidity recovers.

Radian’s earnings call painted a picture of a mortgage insurer in strong financial shape, now layering on a bold specialty insurance platform through Inigo. While rising defaults, tighter holding company liquidity and short‑term accounting uncertainties warrant attention, the overall message was one of confidence in continued earnings power, disciplined capital management and enhanced long‑term growth prospects.

data.title: Zoomcar Announces Private Placement of Common Stock Warrants
data.description:
President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Zoomcar Holdings ( (ZCAR) ) has shared an announcement.

On February 19, 2026, Zoomcar Holdings, Inc. launched a private placement to offer common stock purchase warrants to verified accredited investors under an exemption from U.S. securities registration requirements. Each warrant is exercisable for one share of common stock at an initial exercise price of $6,000 per share, with the securities offered as unregistered instruments subject to applicable transfer restrictions.

Following the issuance of these warrants, the company plans to initiate a separate issuer exchange and/or tender offer allowing eligible accredited holders to exchange warrants for common shares at a currently anticipated ratio of one share for every 20,000 warrants, though terms may change before launch. The contemplated exchange offer would also rely on private-offering exemptions, with any resulting common shares issued as restricted stock and subject to lock-up and other contractual transfer limitations, underscoring a capital structure move that targets sophisticated investors without creating interdependence between the two transactions.

data.description:

Artiva Biotherapeutics, Inc. ( (ARTV) ) has shared an update.

On February 18, 2026, Artiva Biotherapeutics appointed veteran pharmaceutical executive Elaine Sorg as a Class I director, with her term running until the company’s 2028 annual shareholder meeting. The decision followed a recommendation from the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee, and the company noted there were no related-party arrangements or disclosable transactions tied to her selection.

Sorg brings more than 35 years of senior leadership experience at major pharmaceutical groups including AbbVie and Eli Lilly, where she last served as Senior Vice President and President of AbbVie’s U.S. commercial operations until January 2024. Her appointment strengthens Artiva’s board with deep U.S. commercial and global healthcare expertise and is accompanied by standard non-employee director compensation, including an annual cash retainer and time-vested stock options that align her incentives with long-term shareholder interests.

data.title: VSee Health Invests in GoMyRx Digital Prescription Platform
data.description:
President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

VSee Health ( (VSEE) ) has issued an announcement.

On December 26, 2025, VSee Health, Inc. and its subsidiary iDoc Virtual Telehealth Solutions, Inc. entered into a Managed Services Agreement with GoMyRx, Inc., a digital prescription fulfillment platform within the GoMyDocs healthcare ecosystem. Under this agreement, VSee will provide platform administration, customer and user support, third‑party vendor coordination, and reporting and governance services to GoMyRx, billing monthly for actual expenses plus a 10% margin.

The Managed Services Agreement runs through December 26, 2027, with an option for either party to extend it month‑to‑month for up to six additional months, while GoMyRx retains the right to terminate the deal at any time with 20 days’ notice. On January 16, 2026, VSee further deepened its collaboration with GoMyRx by agreeing to purchase $2.0 million of GoMyRx common stock from Go Biz Holdings, LLC in a private transaction, securing a 10% ownership stake and aligning VSee’s strategic and financial interests with the growth of the digital prescription platform.

The most recent analyst rating on (VSEE) stock is a Sell with a $0.33 price target. To see the full list of analyst forecasts on VSee Health stock, see the VSEE Stock Forecast page.

data.description:

  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Lifeward ( (LFWD) ) has shared an update.

On February 12, 2026, Lifeward Ltd. entered into a new secured promissory note with Oramed Pharmaceuticals, Inc. for an initial principal of $525,000, potentially rising by an additional $975,000 by mutual consent. The note, secured by a lien on the company’s cash, carries a 24% annual interest rate and matures on August 12, 2026, or earlier if shareholders fail to approve transactions under previously signed securities and share purchase agreements dated January 12, 2026.

The financing imposes covenants that restrict Lifeward’s ability to incur further debt, grant liens, undertake major corporate changes, or transact with affiliates, and it includes standard default triggers such as non-payment, covenant breaches, insolvency, or a material adverse effect. In an event of default, Oramed can accelerate repayment, raise the interest rate, terminate its commitments, and enforce remedies, while any collateral proceeds will be applied ratably to both the new and existing $3.0 million secured notes, tightening the company’s financial discipline and elevating lender protections.

On February 19, 2026, The York Water Company announced that the Pennsylvania Public Utility Commission approved a joint settlement to revise its water and wastewater base rates, enabling an estimated $18.85 million annual revenue increase. The compensation committee simultaneously overhauled executive long- and short-term incentive plans effective 2026, adding a RevPAR-based performance metric alongside relative total shareholder return, shifting the mix between performance-based and time-based equity, and materially increasing LTIP and bonus targets for the CEO and executive vice presidents, signaling a stronger emphasis on growth, operating performance and shareholder returns despite recent net losses driven by impairments on Non-Core hotels.

Park also reported that in the fourth quarter and full year 2025 it posted adjusted EBITDA of $152 million and $609 million, respectively, while a series of impairment charges tied to Non-Core assets drove net losses of $204 million for the quarter and $277 million for the year, even as Core RevPAR rose and select flagship properties such as Hilton Hawaiian Village and the Bonnet Creek complex delivered robust growth. Over 2025 and into early 2026, the company spent nearly $300 million on renovations, exited or surrendered six Non-Core hotels for more than $132 million in proceeds, expanded and extended its revolving and term loan facilities, and highlighted strong return profiles at recently renovated assets, positioning its streamlined Core portfolio to benefit from expected demand tied to major upcoming events and constrained new supply, while acknowledging macro and geopolitical risks that could temper travel visibility.
